upperAir: The KOAK sounding shows a notable inversion structure: surface temp 11.2°C with dew point spread of only 1.4°C, but by 850mb (1517m) temp has warmed to 12.4°C — a strong subsidence inversion capping thermals near 1500m AGL. The lapse rate from surface to 850mb is only -0.79°C/km (far below dry adiabatic 9.8°C/km), confirming a very stable, thermally suppressed airmass. BL heights in the model data corroborate this, topping out around 350-400m at Ed Levin by early afternoon — thermals will be weak, shallow, and capped well below the inversion. Upper winds are light and variable (210-220° at 700-500mb, 8-9 kts), so no significant wind shear hazard aloft.
aviationFlags: No active AIRMETs for the SF Bay Area region. KSFO reporting FEW006 at 2056Z (600 ft ceiling, marine layer offshore). KHAF persistent OVC014-015 through late afternoon indicating marine layer 1400-1500 ft thick at Half Moon Bay — coastal sites under marine influence. KAPC reporting CLR with 19-21°C and a peak wind gust of 26 kts at 2030Z, confirming active inland heating and valley breeze. KRHV CLR, 22°C, NNW 7 kts — benign at Reid-Hillview. KWVI CLR, 18°C, SSW 7 kts — Watsonville clear and mild.

sites.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft launch)
sites.locationDescriptor: Milpitas — east-facing thermal launch, Santa Clara Valley
sites.howItWorks: Ed Levin 1750 ft is primarily a thermal site that relies on afternoon heating of the Santa Clara Valley to generate convective lift. NW-to-W sea breeze arriving from the bay provides the ideal cross-country window. Wind direction ideally W to NW (270-320°) at 8-15 kts for soarable conditions.
sites.todaySetup: Today's main story at the high launch is a stable, inversion-capped airmass that will severely limit thermal quality — the KOAK sounding shows a subsidence inversion right around 1500m, and BL heights max out around 365m by 2 PM, barely above the launch elevation itself. That said, surface winds build from near-calm in the morning to a usable W-NW 7-9 kts by mid-to-late afternoon (HRRR and GFS in strong agreement), which could support some light ridge soaring or short sledder flights. Thermals will be weak and punchy given the sharp capping inversion — expect broken, turbulent lift rather than organized columns. Wind80 values reaching 9-10 kts by 3-4 PM suggest some gradient-driven flow is arriving, but the thermal ceiling is so low that extended XC is not realistic. KAPC's 26 kt peak gust around 2030Z hints that valley heating was energetic enough to drive the sea breeze onshore, but the sounding inversion is keeping convective development suppressed. Zero precipitation and clear skies overhead make this workable for experienced pilots willing to accept shallow conditions.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Wind nearly calm to 2 kts, variable NW — BL only 45-270m. No thermal or ridge lift available. Not worth launching.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Wind builds slowly to 5-6 kts NW-W. BL reaching ~365m — marginal thermal activity possible but weak and capped. Short sled rides only.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Best window: 3-5 PM with W-NW 7-9 kts at 10m and 8-10 kts at 80m. Consistent direction 284-301°. Light ridge soaring possible but thermals remain shallow under inversion. Wind tapers after 6 PM.

verdict.hazards: Subsidence inversion caps BL near 365m — thermals weak and punchy. Near-calm morning creates rotor risk if wind cycles. KAPC peak gust 26 kts suggests potential for gusty sea breeze arrival.

sites.name: Ed Levin (600 ft launch)
sites.locationDescriptor: Milpitas — lower thermal/training launch, Santa Clara Valley
sites.howItWorks: The 600 ft launch is a lower, more forgiving entry point that works on similar W-NW valley breeze but is better suited to intermediate pilots when conditions are light. Works as a thermal and sledder site; landing area is immediately below.
sites.todaySetup: The lower launch sees slightly better and more consistent wind flow than the high launch during the morning, showing 5-6 kts NW from early on — but this is still below the threshold for reliable soaring given the deeply suppressed BL (75-155m at 8-9 AM). By midday, winds build to 8-9 kts at 300-303° with BL reaching 340-375m, which is adequate for short thermal flights from this elevation. The afternoon window from 2-5 PM with 9-10 kts NW-NNW is the most viable period. Wind80 values of 10-11 kts suggest a reasonably organized gradient aloft. No precipitation, clear skies. The main limitation is the same capping inversion — expect low, scratchy flights rather than extended soaring. The 600 ft launch is the better choice today given lower wind requirements and more consistent NW flow versus the more variable high launch.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: 5-6 kts NW, but BL only 75-290m. Flyable as a short sled ride; no soaring likely. Conditions honest for student observation only.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Wind 8-9 kts, direction 289-303° NW. BL 340-375m — low but marginal for thermal scratching above launch. Short flights possible.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Best window 3-5 PM: 9-10 kts NW-NNW at surface, 10-11 kts at 80m. Clean direction, zero cloud, BL ~345-355m. Workable for P2+ with instructor oversight.

verdict.hazards: Very low BL ceiling limits climb potential. Punchy thermals under inversion cap. Late afternoon NNW shift to 318-325° may push slightly cross.

sites.name: Mussel Rock
sites.locationDescriptor: Daly City — coastal ridge, W-facing, marine layer site
sites.howItWorks: Mussel Rock is a pure ridge soaring site powered by the Pacific onshore flow. It needs clean W-WNW wind at 10-20 kts to generate sustained ridge lift along the coastal bluff. Marine layer keeps it socked in when too thick, but the site can be excellent when the layer is shallow enough for the wind to punch through and stay consistent.
sites.todaySetup: Today is a NO FLY at Mussel Rock — the marine layer is the decisive factor. KHAF is reporting OVC014-015 (1400-1500 ft ceiling) persistently through at least 2135Z, and KSFO had FEW006 (600 ft) at 2056Z, confirming the marine layer is thick, low, and showing no signs of burning off this late in the day. The Half Moon Bay buoy SST is only 13.6°C with a 12-second wave period, indicating a deep, cold-water-fed marine layer with no thermal energy to drive burn-off. GFS and HRRR both agree on 100% cloud cover at Mussel Rock all day, with winds building to 11-13 kts W-WNW by afternoon — the wind direction and speed would normally be ideal, but flying under 1400 ft OVC in coastal fog is a clear no-go. Visibility at the ridge itself is likely compromised, and launching into overcast with potential fog wisps creates serious disorientation risk.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: 100% overcast, OVC014 confirmed at KHAF. Wind 6-9 kts W-WNW — direction fine but ceiling too low. No fly.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Marine layer persists at 100% cloud cover per both models. Wind 8-10 kts WNW — flyable strength but zero ceiling clearance. No fly.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Cloud cover may briefly drop to 77% at 3 PM per GFS, but returns to 100% at 4 PM onward. Wind 11-13 kts W. No viable window — layer too persistent.

verdict.wind: 11 kts W (direction fine, ceiling too low)
verdict.hazards: OVC014-015 marine layer at KHAF all day, KSFO FEW006 — flying into/under coastal overcast, fog, disorientation risk. Cold SST 13.6°C and 12s wave period = deep persistent marine layer, no burn-off expected.
verdict.skillLevel: P3+ locals only (moot — no fly today)

sites.name: Mission Peak
sites.locationDescriptor: Fremont — E-facing thermal launch, East Bay hills
sites.howItWorks: Mission Peak works on thermal lift generated by heating of the Fremont/Tri-Valley floor, enhanced by the sea breeze gradient from the Bay. Ideal wind is W-NW at 8-15 kts. The site is exposed to the full sea breeze push and can become strong and turbulent when the gradient tightens.
sites.todaySetup: Mission Peak shares the same stable airmass as Ed Levin — the KOAK inversion at ~1500m and shallow BL (max ~365m) severely cap thermal development. The site shows near-identical wind profile to Ed Levin: near-calm through late morning, building to 5-9 kts W-NW by afternoon. The BL heights at Mission Peak track closely with Ed Levin, peaking around 365m at 2 PM before settling back. What distinguishes Mission Peak today is that the wind at 120m (wind120_kn) builds impressively to 10-11.6 kts by 2-4 PM, suggesting a stronger gradient aloft even as surface winds are moderate — this height gradient indicates potential for turbulence and punchy thermals on the upper slopes. KRHV reports CLR and 22°C with 7 kts NNW, confirming the valley is heating and driving the sea breeze, but the capping inversion means thermals won't develop into anything soarable. A marginal afternoon window exists for experienced pilots.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Near calm to 2 kts, variable. BL 45-270m. No soarable conditions. Site not worth visiting.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Wind 5-6 kts NW, BL 330-365m. Weak, capped thermals may briefly form. Marginal for experienced pilots; expect short scratchy flights.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Best window 3-5 PM: 7-9 kts W-WNW at 10m, 8-10 kts at 80m. Wind120 reaching 10-12 kts — vertical wind gradient notable. Workable but turbulent under inversion.

verdict.hazards: Strong vertical wind gradient (surface 8 kts vs 120m 11-12 kts) suggests rotor/shear potential. Shallow BL under subsidence inversion — thermals punchy and poorly organized. Not suitable for P2s.

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Tuesday looks similar to today but slightly windier. GFS/HRRR show W-NW 6-10 kts all day, building to 8-10 kts by afternoon. BL heights reach 335m — still capped. Tomorrow.io shows moderate clouds (38-71%) and visibility dropping to 13-15 km with gusts to 9 kts. Overall marginal again; afternoon window 2-5 PM similar to today.

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Similar to high launch — NW 6-10 kts, BL 280-340m. Tomorrow.io flags visibility as low as 7.5 km at 600 ft launch with 77% cloud cover at noon, which warrants watching. Afternoon clearing likely improves the window slightly. Marginal P2+ conditions.

tomorrow.name: Mussel Rock
tomorrow.analysis: Marine layer remains fully entrenched Tuesday per both models — 100% cloud cover all day, wind 12-13 kts WNW which is actually strong and on-shore. Tomorrow.io shows visibility as low as 0.2 km at 10 AM (fog). NO FLY again Tuesday.

tomorrow.name: Mission Peak
tomorrow.analysis: Tuesday marginal, mirroring Ed Levin. NW 6-10 kts, BL capped ~335m. Tomorrow.io shows 88-100% cloud cover with only 3 km visibility through the day — this is notable and may indicate marine layer intrusion into the Fremont hills. Worth checking morning METARs before committing.

day2.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
day2.analysis: Wednesday (Apr 8) looks potentially better. BL heights jump dramatically to 800-1150m by midday-afternoon per GFS, suggesting the inversion breaks. Wind 5-9 kts NW-NNW, CAPE 0 but BL heights indicate real thermal development. Best day of the 4-day period for the high launch.

day2.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
day2.analysis: Wednesday BL heights reach 800-1150m — a major improvement. Wind 5-9 kts NNW, no precip. Should be a genuine thermal day. Best window midday to 4 PM.

day2.name: Mussel Rock
day2.analysis: Wednesday shows a dramatic shift — cloud cover drops to 0-41%, winds back to W-WSW 7-9 kts, and the marine layer appears to thin significantly. This could be a flyable day at Mussel Rock if the clearing holds. Wind direction shifting slightly south (261-265°) may affect lift quality — monitor.

day2.name: Mission Peak
day2.analysis: Wednesday BL heights reaching 740-985m make this the best thermal day of the period. Wind 4-6 kts W-NW with 80m winds building to 10-12 kts by afternoon. Good XC potential if BL heights verify. CAPE still 0 so no OD risk.

day3.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
day3.analysis: Thursday (Apr 9) data not available beyond Day 2 in the provided dataset. Trend from Wednesday suggests continued improvement if high pressure holds.

day3.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
day3.analysis: No Day 3 data available. Follow Wednesday's trend — if high pressure strengthens, Thursday could be excellent.

day3.name: Mussel Rock
day3.analysis: No Day 3 data available. Wednesday's marine layer thinning is encouraging — Thursday could offer a clean window if the pattern continues.

day3.name: Mission Peak
day3.analysis: No Day 3 data available. Wednesday's strong BL development suggests the pattern is improving. Watch for sea breeze timing and gradient strength Thursday.

watchlist: Marine layer at KHAF persistent OVC014-015 with no burn-off signal — cold SST 13.6°C and 12s wave period indicate deep layer. Mussel Rock likely socked in through at least Wednesday morning.
KOAK sounding shows strong subsidence inversion at 850mb (~1500m) keeping BL heights at 330-365m today — thermals at Ed Levin and Mission Peak will be weak and punchy despite clear skies inland.
KAPC peak gust 26 kts at 2030Z today suggests the sea breeze front can arrive energetically in the afternoon — watch for gusty sea breeze onset at Ed Levin 3-5 PM window.
Tomorrow.io shows very low visibility (0.2 km) at Mussel Rock Tuesday 10 AM and 3-7 km at Mission Peak all day Tuesday — possible marine layer intrusion into East Bay hills worth monitoring.
Wednesday shows dramatically higher BL heights (800-1150m) and clearer skies across all sites — mark it as the best flying day of the 4-day period.
tldr: Today's best bet is Ed Levin 600 ft launch with a 3-5 PM window in 9 kts NW wind, but manage expectations — a capping inversion keeps thermals shallow and punchy, so it's sled rides and scratching rather than XC. Mussel Rock is a hard no today (OVC014 marine fog all day). Circle Wednesday on your calendar — BL heights jump to 1100m and skies clear, shaping up as the real flying day this week.
